actions.thought: 76o is a very weak starting hand, even 3-handed on the button. Folding is the standard and correct play here to conserve chips early in the tournament.

Pocket eights is a strong starting hand, especially in a three-handed game. Since the button folded, I am only competing against the Big Blind. I will raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to build the pot and exert pressure, recognizing that I will be out of position for the rest of the hand if called.

Holding 6d 5d in the Big Blind against a Small Blind raise, I have favorable pot odds to see a flop. Suited connectors play well in blind-versus-blind scenarios, providing potential for straights and flushes. Given the raise size and position, calling is the most standard and profitable approach, as 3-betting would bloat a pot with a hand that is better played cautiously post-flop.

I have flopped middle set (8c 8s on 9c 8d Jd). This is an extremely strong hand, but the board is very wet with numerous straight and flush draws. I need to bet for value and to charge opponents for their draws. Checking would be too dangerous on such a coordinated board. I will lead out with a bet of 400, which is approximately two-thirds of the pot, to build the pot and protect my hand.

The flop is highly coordinated (9c 8d Jd), and holding 6d 5d gives me a flush draw and a gutshot straight draw, offering significant equity. With 1000 in the pot and a 400 bet, I am receiving 3.5-to-1 pot odds, making a call mathematically profitable. Calling allows me to realize my equity, control the pot size in this early stage, and re-evaluate on the turn.

The turn card (Qh) is quite dangerous as it completes many possible straights (any Ten now has a straight). While I have a set of eights, the board of 9-8-J-Q is extremely coordinated. Flashlight's range for calling pre-flop and on the flop includes many hands containing a Ten (like T9, JT, QT). By checking, I am controlling the pot size and avoiding a large commitment against a potentially better hand, while still leaving room to call a reasonable bet or see a river card to potentially fill up to a full house.

The board is highly coordinated (9c 8d Jd Qh), and my 6d 5d provides a flush draw. Since the opponent checked, taking the free card is a standard, high-equity play. Betting here as a semi-bluff might be aggressive, but checking ensures I see the river for free, allowing me to realize my draw's equity without inflating the pot against a potentially trapping hand or a completed straight.

The river King completes a board (9-8-J-Q-K) where any Ten makes a straight. While I have a set of eights, this hand has lost significant value given the high probability that Flashlight holds a Ten or has improved to a higher two-pair or set. After the turn and river development, checking is the most prudent move to control the pot size and see if we can reach a cheap showdown, as I am likely beaten by a wide range of hands that called the flop.

The board is 9c-8d-Jd-Qh-Ks, which provides a straight for anyone holding a Ten. My hand, 6d-5d, has failed to improve to a pair or better, leaving me with only King-high (playing the board). Since the opponent checked, they might also be weak, but betting into them with 6-high is a low-percentage bluff that risks unnecessary chips. Checking is the optimal play to reach showdown without further investment.

I am on the button with Q7o in a 3-handed game. While the button opening range is wide, Q7o is generally at the very bottom of that range or slightly outside of it depending on the aggressiveness of the blinds. Given that it is early in the tournament and I want to maintain a solid image, I will choose to fold this marginal hand and wait for a better spot rather than risking chips with a weak Queen-high offsuit hand.

With 9c 4c in the small blind and the button having folded, I am in a heads-up situation against the big blind. While 94s is a weak hand, it is a suited connector with reasonable playability. Given that I have already posted 50, it is standard to complete the remaining 50 to see a flop and realize my equity, rather than folding and conceding the pot immediately.

I am in the big blind facing a limp from the small blind. I hold a very weak hand, 83 offsuit. There is no reason to invest extra chips by raising as a bluff, so checking my option to see a free flop and realize my equity is the standard and correct play.

The board is monotone with three spades (8s 6s 3s), and I have no spade flush draw with my 9c4c. Betting into the big blind here would be purely bluffing on a board that likely hits a wide defending range. Checking is the standard play to control the pot size out of position and evaluate how the opponent reacts, while avoiding committing chips with no equity.

I flopped top and bottom pair on a dangerous monotone board (8s 6s 3s) without holding a spade. Flashlight checked to me. While my two pair is likely the best hand right now, it is extremely vulnerable to flush draws (any single spade) and straight draws. Betting is necessary to protect my equity by denying free cards and to extract value from worse hands like a single high spade, a weaker pair, or a straight draw. A half-pot bet of 100 is a good size to accomplish this without overcommitting in case my opponent has already flopped a flush.

The flop is heavily coordinated with three spades, and I hold 9c4c with no pair and no spade. My opponent has bet into me, and I have no equity to justify a call or a raise. Continuing in this hand with 9-high on such a wet board would be an unnecessary waste of chips. Folding is the most prudent move to conserve my stack.
Queen-four offsuit is a weak hand that does not play well in multi-way pots. With two opponents to act behind me, folding is the most prudent decision to conserve stack size.
With KTo in the Small Blind and the Button folded, I have a strong enough hand to open-raise to try and steal the Big Blind or play heads-up. Raising to 300 (3bb) is a standard open size.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7d 4c, a very weak offsuit hand. The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. While I am getting 2:1 odds on a call, 74o has poor equity and playability post-flop, even in a blind-vs-blind battle. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

5h 3h is a weak suited one-gapper. While we are on the button in a 3-handed game, this hand is still quite marginal and can lead to tricky post-flop situations. Folding is a solid, low-variance play early in the tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Seven offsuit. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Flashlight). In a Blind vs. Blind scenario, K7o is a relatively strong hand. To capitalize on this and potentially win the pot immediately or take control of the hand, I will raise. Being out of position post-flop, I prefer a raise to exert pressure. I will raise to 300 total.

I hold Queen-Jack offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a strong hand for defending against a Small Blind open. While a 3-bet is possible, it is early in the tournament and keeping the pot size manageable while playing in position post-flop is the prudent strategic approach.

I raised from the Small Blind pre-flop and am now heads-up against the Big Blind. The flop of 3c-2h-4h is low and coordinated, which often connects well with a Big Blind's calling range (containing many low cards, connectors, and suited hands). However, as the pre-flop aggressor, I want to maintain my initiative. A continuation bet of half the pot (300 chips) allows me to represent a strong overpair or an Ace while potentially forcing a fold from hands that missed the flop. If I am called or raised, I will have to proceed with caution on later streets as my King-high has limited showdown value.

The board is extremely low and coordinated (2-3-4). My hand, QJ high, has completely missed and lacks any significant draw or equity against a range that connects well with this texture. Calling here would be a mistake as my hand offers no showdown value and minimal chance to improve to the best hand against an opponent betting into a small blind open. Folding preserves my stack for a more favorable situation.

I am on the button with 7-8 offsuit. In 3-handed play, this is a marginal but acceptable hand to open-raise with to apply pressure on the blinds. A standard 3x raise (300 chips) is a good way to test the blinds and potentially win the pot immediately or play it in position post-flop.

AQo is a strong hand in a 3-handed game, and playing it from the small blind against a button open is best handled with a 3-bet to take the initiative and avoid playing out of position. Raising to 900 creates pressure on the button and builds a pot while I have the lead.

I am in the Big Blind holding Q4s. The Button opened to 300 and the Small Blind 3-bet to 900. My hand is extremely weak and highly dominated in this 3-bet pot. The only standard and correct play here is to fold.

I opened from the button with 7-8 offsuit, which is a marginal hand. Flashlight in the small blind has 3-bet me to 900. While I am in position, 7-8 offsuit does not play well against a 3-betting range, which likely includes strong pairs and high cards. Continuing by calling 600 more would put me in a difficult spot post-flop with a weak hand in a bloated pot. The best disciplined play here is to fold and conserve my stack.

In a three-handed game, K9o is a strong enough hand to open from the button. Raising to 2.5x the big blind is a standard opening size to capture the blinds and maintain initiative, especially against two opponents.
I am dealt 3d 8s in the Small Blind. This is a very weak starting hand with negative expected value, especially facing a button raise from Flashlight. The optimal and standard play is to fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Ten offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has opened for 250, and the Small Blind has folded. ATo is a very strong hand in a 3-handed context against a standard button opening range. While calling is an option to close the pre-flop action, 3-betting allows me to take the initiative and potentially win the pot immediately. I will raise to 800 to put pressure on Flashlight's range and define the strength of their hand.

K9o is a marginal opening hand in a three-handed game, but it performs poorly against a 3-bet. Flash has re-raised my initial open to 800, indicating significant strength. Calling 550 to see a flop with a dominated hand like K9o is negative expected value in the long run. Given the stack depths and the aggressive nature of the 3-bet, folding is the most disciplined and prudent action to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

We are dealt King-Jack offsuit on the button in a 3-handed game. This is a strong starting hand. Opening with a raise allows us to apply pressure to the blinds, potentially winning the pot right now, or playing a heads-up pot with positional advantage. A standard 2.5x open to 250 is optimal.

With 3h 2h in the Small Blind, I am facing a 2.5x open from the Button. While the hand is suited, it is the lowest possible suited connector and performs poorly out of position. Calling would often lead to difficult post-flop scenarios or a squeeze from the Big Blind. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my chip lead.

Jack-Ten offsuit is a standard, profitable hand to defend from the big blind against a button open. With 2.6-to-1 pot odds (150 to call into a 400 pot), calling is mathematically sound and allows me to see a flop with a decent holding that plays well in position-agnostic scenarios.

I am out of position against the pre-flop raiser (Pro) on a relatively dry board of Qd-4d-2s. My hand, Jack-Ten offsuit, has missed the flop entirely, holding only a backdoor straight draw and a single diamond. Checking is the standard, disciplined play when out of position to see if the aggressor continues, as leading into them with such a weak holding would be difficult to navigate.

We have King-high on a Qd 4d 2s board. The opponent checked to us. Since we were the pre-flop aggressor and block some top-pair hands (KQ, QJ), a continuation bet of around 1/3 to 1/2 pot is a standard play to fold out better high-card hands or underpairs. Flashlight has shown a tendency to fold to flop bets when missing, so a 200 chip bet is a profitable bluff.

The opponent has bet 200 into a 500 pot, giving me pot odds of 3.5-to-1 (200 to call into a total pot of 900). Holding J-T with the Jack of diamonds provides me with a backdoor flush draw and two overcards to the small cards on the board. Given the relatively dry texture of the Qd-4d-2s flop, the opponent's bet is likely a standard continuation bet rather than a made hand. Calling here is mathematically profitable and allows me to see a turn card to re-evaluate my equity.

I am in the Big Blind and hold Jack-Ten of diamonds. The board is Queen-Four-Deuce-King, giving me an open-ended straight draw (needing an Ace or a Nine) plus a backdoor flush draw. As the first to act, checking is the most strategic play to keep the pot controlled while realizing my equity. Betting here would be a pure semi-bluff, but given that the pot is already 950, checking allows me to see a river card for free, which is statistically advantageous with 8 strong straight outs.

The turn brings a King, giving us top pair with a strong Jack kicker. Flashlight check-called the flop, suggesting a range that includes a Queen, middle pairs, or flush draws. Their turn check indicates they likely haven't improved to a hand that beats us. Betting here is crucial to extract value from a stubborn Queen or weaker pairs, while also charging any diamond flush draws to see the river. A half-pot sizing of 500 chips achieves both goals effectively.

I hold Jack-Ten of diamonds on a board of Queen-Four-Deuce-King. This gives me an open-ended straight draw (needing an Ace or a Nine to complete the straight). The pot is 1450, and the opponent has bet 500, offering pot odds of approximately 3.9-to-1. With 8 outs, my probability of hitting the straight on the river is about 17.4%. While the direct pot odds are slightly unfavorable, the implied odds of hitting the straight against an aggressive opponent, combined with the fact that hitting an Ace or Nine likely provides me with the best hand, make calling the mathematically superior play to realize my equity.

I hold Jack-high on a board of Qd-4d-2s-Kc-7d. I have no pair, no flush, and no straight. The pot is 1950, and the Pro has shown aggression on both the flop and the turn. Given that I am out of position and have no showdown value, betting would be an ill-advised bluff against a range that likely hit the King or Queen. Checking is the only disciplined and standard play here to minimize losses.

The river brings the 7 of diamonds, completing the obvious flush draw that could easily be in my opponent's flop and turn check-calling range. While I have top pair with a Jack kicker, betting here is thin because it is unlikely a worse hand, like a bare Queen, will call a third barrel on this scary board. Meanwhile, all better hands (flushes, two pairs) will call or raise. Checking back allows me to safely realize my showdown value without risking more chips.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. I have a healthy stack of over 100 big blinds. My standard strategy from the button is to raise to 3x the big blind (300) to build the pot, exert pressure on the blinds, and take advantage of my positional advantage for the rest of the hand.

8d9d is a strong suited connector that performs well in multi-way pots and against a button open. With a 3x open from the button, calling to see a flop is a standard play that balances my range and gives me the opportunity to realize equity with a hand that has good post-flop potential. I prefer calling over 3-betting here to keep the pot size manageable while out of position, and folding would be too tight given the price and hand strength.

6d 3d is a very weak, disconnected suited hand. Even though I am getting decent pot odds (200 to call into a pot of 700) and closing the action, playing this hand out of position multiway will mostly lead to difficult situations and reverse implied odds. Folding is the standard and safest play.

The board (3s-Qs-Ts) is highly unfavorable for my holding of 8d-9d. I have no pair, no flush draw, and no straight draw, essentially holding 8-high with almost no equity. Leading into the pre-flop aggressor (the Button) would be a tactical error as it accomplishes nothing. The standard play is to check, and if the opponent bets, I will likely have to fold unless the price is extremely attractive, given my lack of drawing potential.

I have top pair, top kicker with Ace-Queen on a monotone board. Although the three spades are concerning since I do not hold one, I likely still have the best hand against Flashlight's calling range from the small blind. I need to bet for value against weaker Queens (like QJ or Q9) and to charge any hands containing a single spade looking to draw to a flush. Flashlight's check provides an opportunity to take the lead, and a bet of roughly 60% of the pot is a standard continuation to protect my equity.

The board (3s-Qs-Ts) is highly coordinated, providing significant equity to many hands in the opponent's range. Holding 8d-9d, I have essentially no equity, as I lack a pair, a flush draw, and a straight draw. Calling a bet here would be burning chips with no realistic path to winning the hand. Given the texture of the board and my complete lack of connection, folding is the only prudent decision to preserve my stack.

With K-10 offsuit in a 3-handed game from the button, I have the positional advantage. This is a strong enough hand to open the pot to build value, as it dominates many hands that the blinds might defend with. Raising to 300 (3x the big blind) is a standard open to take the initiative.

Facing a standard 3x button open from Flashlight, pocket threes are an excellent candidate to call for set-mining. Our effective stacks (7500 with Flashlight) provide sufficient implied odds, well over the 15x rule of thumb needed to profitably mine for sets. We will play fit-or-fold post-flop, folding if we miss our set and face aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Five of Spades. Flashlight opened from the button for 300, and Pro called from the Small Blind. I need to call 200 more into a pot that will be 900, giving me 4.5:1 pot odds. K5s is a strong enough hand to defend in this spot, especially with the potential to flop a flush draw or top pair. While I will be out of position, the price is too good to pass up.

We flopped an absolute monster: a full house, threes full of sixes! Being first to act in the small blind, the best play is to check to the pre-flop raiser. This allows Flashlight to continuation bet with their entire range, including overcards and bluffs, and gives Flash a chance to stab at the pot. Leading out here would likely fold out their weaker holdings and air, so we want to trap and build the pot gradually.
I missed the flop with King-high. On a paired board like 6-3-6, there's no reason to lead out into two opponents. I will check and see how the button (the pre-flop aggressor) reacts.

The board (6d, 3h, 6h) is relatively dry and does not connect well with the pre-flop calling ranges of the blinds. As the original raiser in position, checking behind gives free cards to both opponents, allowing them to realize their equity or hit their draws. With King-high, I am likely ahead of their range here. A small continuation bet of 400 is appropriate to test their strength, potentially taking down the pot immediately or narrowing their ranges if called.

With our flopped full house (threes full of sixes), we have an absolute monster. Flashlight has made a standard continuation bet of 400 into a pot of 900. Since we are in a multiway pot, check-raising now might look incredibly strong, potentially folding out all of Flashlight's bluffs and marginal hands like overcards, while also forcing Flash out of the pot. By smooth calling, we keep Flash in the hand if they have a piece or a draw, and we give Flashlight enough rope to continue bluffing or value betting weaker holdings on the turn.

I have King-high on a paired board of 6-3-6. Flashlight (the pre-flop aggressor) has bet 400 into a 900 pot, and Pro has called. While the pot odds are quite favorable (400 to call for a total pot of 2100), my hand has very little equity in a three-way pot. Pro's call suggests he has at least a pair or a heart draw. Even if I hit a King, I could still be behind. I will fold and conserve my chips.

We still have a monster hand, threes full of sixes, on a very safe board. The turn card is the Jack of diamonds, which introduces a new flush draw but is otherwise harmless. It might even connect with Flashlight's range (e.g., AJ, KJ, QJ, JTs), giving them top pair and encouraging them to bet again for value. By checking, we continue to disguise the strength of our hand and give Flashlight the opportunity to fire a second barrel with overpairs, top pair, or flush draws. If they bet, we can strongly consider check-raising to start playing for their entire stack.

The board (6d, 3h, 6h, Jd) remains relatively dry, and Pro calling my flop bet suggests they likely have a piece of the board, such as a six, a pocket pair, or a flush draw. With only King-high, my hand is currently weak, and continuing to bluff into a player who has shown resistance is high-risk. Checking behind is the most prudent strategy to control the pot size and take a free card to the river, potentially realizing showdown value if the river checks through.

We have a strong full house, threes full of sixes. Flashlight checked behind on the turn, indicating either pot control with a marginal made hand (like a Jack or medium pocket pair) or a draw. The river 8 of hearts completes the flop's heart flush draw. If we check, it is highly likely Flashlight will check back to realize their showdown value, and we will miss out on value. Leading out for around half the pot targets calls from hands like a Jack, completed flushes, or straights, and also leaves room for Flashlight to raise if they rivered a strong flush.

The board (6d, 3h, 6h, Jd, 8h) is quite wet, with the river flush draw completing. My opponent, Pro, check-called the flop and checked the turn, then decided to lead out on the river for 900 into a 1700 pot. With only King-high, I have no showdown value against any pair or made flush. The bet size represents value from a made hand (a six, a jack, or an eight) or a flush. Given the board texture and the action, calling with King-high is a losing play as I am essentially a bluff-catcher against a range that likely has the board hit. Folding is the most disciplined and statistically sound decision here.
J8 offsuit is a weak hand to open with, even from the button. Folding is the most standard and solid choice to preserve my stack.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Nine offsuit after the Button has folded. K9o is a strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. I have the opportunity to take control of the hand or win the pot immediately. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to pressure the Big Blind, who has a shorter stack of 7,000 compared to my 10,900. Raising is superior to limping here as it allows me to define the Big Blind's range and potentially win the blinds uncontested.

I am in the big blind facing a raise from the small blind. My hand, Q8 offsuit, is a standard defending hand in a heads-up scenario given the pot odds provided. The raise size is relatively small, making a call profitable to see a flop in position against the small blind's opening range.

I was the pre-flop aggressor from the Small Blind. The flop of 5d-5s-Jh is relatively dry, meaning it's unlikely to have hit the Big Blind's calling range hard unless they have a Jack or a Five. By placing a continuation bet of 300 (50% of the pot), I maintain the initiative and pressure the opponent to fold their air and speculative hands. This bet size is large enough to fold out hands like 67s or 89s while not risking too much of my stack if I am raised.

I am holding Queen-high on a paired board (5-5-J). My hand has very little equity against any part of the opponent's betting range, which includes Jacks, fives, and pocket pairs. With no meaningful draw and being out of position, continuing here would be burning chips. Folding is the most prudent decision.

I am on the button with 85o. While 3-handed play warrants a wide opening range from the button, 85o is at the very bottom of the range and generally considered a fold. Looking at previous hands (Hand 8 and Hand 10), Flashlight in the Small Blind has shown a tendency to call my button raises. Since 85o performs poorly against a calling range, I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a better spot to apply pressure.

With the Button folding, I am in the Small Blind against the Big Blind. Queen-Nine offsuit is a playable hand in this heads-up scenario. Raising to 300 is a standard move to apply pressure and potentially win the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand going into the flop.

I hold pocket Deuces in the Big Blind facing a 3x open from the Small Blind. Given our deep effective stack (Flashlight has around 6500 behind) and my positional advantage post-flop, calling to set-mine is profitable. Hitting a set occurs about 12% of the time, and the implied odds are favorable. If I miss the flop and face aggression, I can easily fold.

I have flopped trip nines, which is a very strong hand on this dry board. There is a potential flush draw present, but my trip nines are dominant. I want to build the pot and extract value from the opponent's weaker holdings such as pocket pairs, lower pairs, or draws. Betting 400 into the 600 pot is a standard continuation that keeps the pot growing while maintaining control of the hand.

I missed my set on the flop, leaving me with the lowest possible pocket pair on a paired board (9s 3h 9h). Flashlight's continuation bet of 400 into a 600 pot indicates strength or a standard c-bet, but my pocket deuces are highly vulnerable to almost their entire range, including any higher pair, a 9, or even overcards. As planned pre-flop, since I didn't hit my set and am facing aggression, the most profitable decision is to simply fold and preserve my stack.

Ten-three offsuit is an extremely weak hand with poor playability, even in a late position like the button. There is no incentive to enter the pot with such a marginal holding, so folding is the disciplined choice to preserve my stack for more favorable opportunities.

With the Button folding, I am in the Small Blind with K9s. This is a strong suited hand for a blind versus blind situation. Raising to 300 puts pressure on the Big Blind and frequently wins the pot outright, while still having good playability post-flop if called.

I am in the Big Blind with a pocket pair of sevens (7h 7s). The Small Blind (Pro) has raised to 300. In a blind-versus-blind scenario, pocket sevens is a very strong hand. Additionally, as the Big Blind, I will have position over the Small Blind for the remainder of the hand. I will 3-bet to 900 to build the pot for value and take control of the hand.

We opened K9 suited from the Small Blind as a standard steal attempt. Flash (Big Blind) 3-bet to 900. While K9s is a decent starting hand, calling a 3-bet out of position with it is problematic because we are often dominated by stronger Kings or higher pairs. Folding is the most prudent play here to preserve our stack.

I am on the Button with J6o, a very weak offsuit hand. Being first to act in a 3-handed game, this hand does not have enough equity or playability to warrant an open raise. The standard and most profitable play is to simply fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with 3d 4d. The Button (Pro) has folded, leaving only me and the Big Blind (Flashlight). In 3-handed play, opening ranges from the Small Blind should be quite wide, especially with suited connectors which have decent post-flop playability. I have been playing aggressively in previous hands, and raising here puts pressure on Flashlight, who has a shorter stack. I will raise to 300 to try and win the pot immediately or see a flop with initiative.

I hold Ace-King offsuit, a premium starting hand, while in the big blind against an aggressive opponent in the small blind who has been active throughout the session. By 3-betting to 900, I can take initiative in the hand, bloat the pot while I likely have the equity advantage, and discourage the opponent from seeing a cheap flop with a wide range.

I opened from the Small Blind with a speculative hand (3d 4d) to put pressure on the Big Blind. However, Flashlight responded with a 3-bet to 900. Given that I am out of position and 3d 4d has very low equity against a typical 3-betting range, calling is not profitable. I have previously folded to Flashlight's 3-bet in Hand #5 with a stronger hand (8h 7c), and I will maintain that disciplined approach here by folding.

I am on the button with 94o, which is a very weak hand. While being on the button allows for a wide opening range in a 3-handed game, 94o is typically outside of a profitable opening range, especially given that both opponents have shown moderate aggression in previous hands. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to preserve my stack.

With pocket sevens, I have a strong hand for heads-up play. Since the button folded, I am heads-up against the big blind. Raising allows me to take the betting initiative and define my range, giving me the best chance to win the pot either pre-flop or by continuing aggression on a favorable flop.

Q2o is a very weak starting hand. Even though it's a blind vs. blind situation and I only need to call 200 more, the hand lacks playability, connectivity, and suitedness to profitably defend against a raise from the small blind.

With Ten-Eight suited on the button, I have a playable hand in a late position. Opening with a raise allows me to take control of the hand and potentially steal the blinds, leveraging my position advantage against the small and big blinds.

AhTd is an unsuited Broadway hand. While it has some blocker value and raw equity, playing ATo from the Small Blind facing a 3bb raise from the Button is generally a fold in standard GTO charts. Calling invites the Big Blind to squeeze or overcall, putting us out of position post-flop with a marginal holding. 3-betting is an option, but ATo is usually better as a fold given the sizing and position.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Six offsuit. The Button (Flashlight) has raised to 300, and the Small Blind (Pro) has already folded. To call, I need to put in 200 more into a pot that would then be 650. Jack-Six offsuit is a relatively weak hand, and I will be playing out of position against Flashlight for the rest of the hand. While the price is somewhat attractive (2:1), Flashlight has been fairly active and aggressive in previous hands, and J6o does not play well post-flop or realize its equity easily. I prefer to fold here and wait for a better spot rather than defending with a sub-par hand out of position.

I am on the Button with 97o, an offsuit two-gapper. This hand is too weak to open or limp from this position. Folding is the standard and most profitable play here to conserve my stack for better starting hands.
I am in the Small Blind with QdTd after the Button folded. This is a premium hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure to the Big Blind.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Jack offsuit against a raise from the Small Blind. This is a strong hand that should defend well against an aggressive opponent. While I could 3-bet, calling allows me to see a flop with a range advantage while keeping the pot manageable out of position. AJo is too strong to fold given the current pot odds.

I have hit top pair on the flop with a strong Queen kicker. The board texture is relatively safe, though a club flush draw is present. I will make a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to extract value from weaker Tens, middle pairs, and draws while maintaining my initiative in the hand.

The flop of 3h-Tc-4c is relatively dry, and while I haven't hit a pair, I hold two overcards and the Ace of clubs, giving me decent backdoor flush potential and high card equity. Folding would be too weak, and raising into a potential trap or a made Ten is unnecessary risk. Calling is the standard play here to keep the pot manageable and see a turn card, as it allows me to re-evaluate based on the next card while keeping my range wide.

The turn card is the 4s, which pairs the board but likely doesn't improve my opponent's range unless they are defending with a 4. I still have top pair with a strong kicker (Qd Td). Holding Ace-Four offsuit (A4o) on the button in a 3-handed game is a standard opening hand. With 123.5 big blinds, I have a healthy stack and want to maintain aggression. Raising here allows me to pick up the blinds or play a pot in position. I'll stick to my standard open size of 300 chips.

6-2 offsuit is an extremely weak starting hand. Defending from the small blind against a button raise with such a marginal holding is mathematically unprofitable (negative EV). Folding is the disciplined and correct decision.

I have Ten-high on an Ace-high board that also has potential straight and flush completions. After my flop continuation bet was called, I checked the turn and river to signal a surrender.
